title: Email: a message, a thread, and attachments
audience: Born-digital archives, institutional records, personal papers
intro: Email is born-digital correspondence at scale. The fork is message-vs-thread; the twist is the attachment, which is a distinct digital object, not just a property of the message. This scenario models all three and attaches the sender.
question: Is a single email a Record and a thread a Record Set? And how do I handle attachments?

steps.prompt: Are you describing one message, or a whole thread?

choices.label: A single message
choices.why: Good — one message is one Record.

choices.label: A thread / conversation
choices.why: Good — a thread is an aggregation of messages: a Record Set, with each message a member Record (ordered by send time).

choices.label: An Instantiation
choices.why: Not the starting point. The .eml/.msg file on disk is an Instantiation of a message; first choose the intellectual unit (Record or Record Set).

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. One message = one Record. Sent date and subject line attach as a date and subject; the stored .eml file is an Instantiation; attachments are handled next.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one message — a Set is for the thread.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's the stored file format, not the message's content. Keep the message as a Record.

capture.comment: Create the email message as a Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. The thread is an aggregation of messages — a Record Set — each message a member Record. Reply order is captured by sequence/date relations between the messages.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — a thread is many messages with distinct senders and times. Use a Record Set of message Records.

choices.label: Activity
choices.why: Doesn't fit. Corresponding is an activity, but the thread itself is the aggregated messages — a Record Set.

capture.comment: Create the thread as a Record Set.

choices.label: Agent (Person) + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. The sender is a Person (an Agent). Relate the message to them with has_creator (RiC also has sender/addressee relations; has_creator is the safe general choice). The same Agent is reused for every message that person sent.

choices.label: Leave it in the From: header text
choices.why: Doesn't fit for description. The raw header lives in the stored file, but for a reconcilable, queryable archive the sender should be a first-class Agent.

capture.comment: Create the sender as an Agent.
Link the message/thread to the sender with has_creator.

steps.prompt: The message had a spreadsheet attached. How do you model the attachment?

choices.label: Record Part of the message
choices.why: Fits well. An attachment is a distinct piece of content carried within the message — a Record Part contributes to the message's completeness while keeping its own format, title and (sometimes) author. Its stored file is then an Instantiation of that Record Part.

choices.label: Instantiation of the message
choices.why: Doesn't fit — an Instantiation is a carrier of the SAME content (the .eml file is one), but the spreadsheet is different content travelling with the message, not another rendering of the email body.

choices.label: A column on the message
choices.why: Too flat. Attachments have their own filenames, formats, sizes and sometimes distinct creators — they deserve to be described entities (Record Parts).

capture.comment: Create the attachment as a Record Part of the message.

outcome.verdict: Message, thread, sender and attachment — all first-class.
outcome.summary: A message is a Record; a thread is a Record Set of messages; the sender is a reusable Agent; and the attachment is a Record Part with its own file. Born-digital correspondence becomes a graph you can search by person, date and content — not an opaque mailbox dump.

links.label: Record Part (RiC-E05) in the model navigator
See real RiC data in the graph viewer
